steps.title: Separate the two effects
steps.content: The researchers predicted two effects from positive feedback in the virtual cycling task. One effect was psychological: participants would have a better experience of the activity. The other was physical: participants would show better endurance, measured by how long they sustained the target pedaling and heart rates.

steps.title: Reject the wrong comparisons
steps.content: Choice B focuses on negative feedback, so it does not show what positive feedback did on both measures. Choice C says participants who received positive feedback maintained their rates for longer than the neutral group, which would support a physical endurance effect. Choice D compares neutral feedback with positive feedback for enjoyment but compares neutral feedback with negative feedback for duration, so it does not isolate the needed result.

steps.title: Choose enjoyment without endurance
steps.content: The needed finding must show positive feedback helping psychological experience but not improving physical endurance. A result where the positive-feedback group reports greater enjoyment shows the psychological effect. A result where that same group maintains heart and pedaling rates for approximately similar durations as the other groups shows no physical endurance advantage. The answer is A.
